revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:二塚古墳]]
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} (non-existent) | name = Futatsuka Kofun | location = Shikano 2-chome, Taihaku-ku, Sendai, Miyagi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|38|13|47.6|N|140|52|20.1|E|display=inline,title}} | built = Middle or Late Kofun period | area = Total length: 20-30m (estimated) | Interred_individual = Unknown | notes = None | burial goods = Sarcophagus, Sue pottery | image = Coffin-FutatsukaKofun.jpg |caption=Sarcophagus discovered at the site}}
'''Futatsuka Kofun''' (二塚古墳) was a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} located in Shikano 2-chome, Taihaku-ku, Sendai, Miyagi Prefecture. It was constructed during the middleor late {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} and measured 20-30 meters in length. In the early 20th century, a Sarcophagus was unearthed at the site, along with fragments of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|qid=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}}. The mound was destroyed in 1949, and no traces remain today.
== Overview == Futatsuka Kofun was situated to the southwest of Sendai's urban center on an alluvial plain south of {{ill|Ōtōjiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtōjiyama|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Daidenji|qq=}}. Approximately 300 meters east of the site stood {{ill|Hitotsuzuka Kofun|ja|一塚古墳|1=Hitotsuka Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitotsuzuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitotsuka Kofun|qq=}}, with {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|兜塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)|qq=}} farther northeast. Locally known as "Futatsuka" or "Futatsuka-yama," the mound's name likely derived from its keyhole-shaped structure, characterized by two distinct raised sections.<ref>Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," p.55.</ref>
At the time of the Sarcophagus's discovery, the mound was part of the typeer Mogasakimura in Natori District. The front square section faced west, measuring approximately 9-11 meters on each side and rising 2 meters high, while the circular rear section was 13 meters in diameter and 2.4 meters high. Alternative estimates suggest a total length of 29 meters and a width of 15 meters.<ref>Earlier area provided by Takano Matsujirō, "Kofun Discovered Near Mogasakimura, Sendai," pp.87-88, differ from later estimates by Fushise Chizō and others.</ref> The mound had no detectable stone chamber, moat, or stairways.
== Discovery of the Stone Coffin == There are multiple accounts of the Sarcophagus's discovery: - In the summer of 1905, a local youth unearthed the coffin while collecting soil for a sumo wrestling ring.<ref>Takano Matsujirō, "Kofun Discovered Near Mogasakimura, Sendai," p.87.</ref> - On March 16, 1906, the coffin was accidentally uncovered.<ref>Fushise Chizō, "A Record of Ancient Kofuns in Mogasakimura, Natori District, Miyagi Prefecture."</ref> - Yoshikichi Aoki, a local farmer, reportedly discovered it while clearing fallow land.<ref name="#1">Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," p.57.</ref>
The coffin, carved from tuff, lacked its lid, although a potential lid stone was found nearby.<ref name="#1"/> Its reported area vary: approximately 2.3-2.4 meters in length, 1 meter in width, and 30 centimeters in depth.<ref>Measurements vary between sources, such as Fushise Chizō and Takano Matsujirō, due to differing methodologies and interpretations.</ref> Two pieces of Sue pottery were also found within.<ref>Takano Matsujirō, "Kofun Discovered Near Mogasakimura, Sendai," p.88.</ref>
== Decline and Destruction == The mound was gradually encroached upon, with farmland established atop it.<ref>Kazai Shinya, "An Overview of Protohistoric Sites in the Ōu Region," p.318.</ref> By 1931, the Sarcophagus remained exposed and neglected.<ref>Shimizu Tōshirō, 'General History of Miyagi Prefecture,' p.5.</ref> In 1949, the mound was leveled, and the site was converted into rice fields.<ref>Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," p.57; "Kofuns in Sendai," p.31.</ref> The area is now a residential neighborhood, with no visible remnants of the kofun.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," in Sendai City History, vol. 3, edited by the Sendai City History Compilation Committee, 1950. * Itō Nobuo, "Kofuns in Sendai," in Studies of Sendai's Local History, Hōbundō, 1979. * Kazai Shinya, "An Overview of Protohistoric Sites in the Ōu Region," Archaeological Journal, vol. 8, no. 6, February 1918. * Shimizu Tōshirō, 'General History of Miyagi Prefecture,' Shin'yakusha Bookstore, 1931. (Reprinted by Hōbundō in 1976.) * Takano Matsujirō, "Kofun Discovered Near Mogasakimura, Sendai," Archaeological World, vol. 6, no. 2, December 1906. * Fushise Chizō, "A Record of Ancient Kofuns in Mogasakimura, Natori District, Miyagi Prefecture," Archaeological World, vol. 6, no. 2, December 1906.
{{Kofun navbar}}
